text: Opening files, post-mortems, prep. Black plays Sicilian. White plays Ruy Lopez or d4.
Rules of the road
Every loss gets a post-mortem before next session. No exceptions.
Najdorf: 1.e4 c5 2.Nf3 d6 3.d4 cxd4 4.Nxd4 Nf6 5.Nc3 a6. Point of a6 is to deny b5 to White's pieces and prep ...e5 or ...e6.
Main tries for White
After 6.Bg5 e6 7.f4 Be7 is the classical main line. 6.Be3 English Attack is sharper, opposite-side castling.
Drill the poisoned pawn line 7...Qb6 ten games on Lichess
English Attack: 6.Be3 e5 7.Nb3 Be6 8.f3 Be7 9.Qd2 O-O 10.O-O-O. Both sides race on opposite wings.
Black gets ...Nbd7, ...b5, ...Nb6 with queenside play. Tempo counts. Do not waste a move.
Memorize the ...b5-b4 vs Nd5 trade and when to take
Nimzo: 1.d4 Nf6 2.c4 e6 3.Nc3 Bb4. Pin the knight, fight for e4. Rubinstein is 4.e3.
Plan as Black: trade on c3, double White's pawns, blockade the c4 square, then play against the structure.
Winawer: 1.e4 e6 2.d4 d5 3.Nc3 Bb4 4.e5 c5 5.a3 Bxc3+ 6.bxc3. White has the bishop pair, Black hits the center.
Bad bishop on c8 is the eternal French problem. Solve it or suffer. ...Qa5 hits c3 and ties White down.
Check the 7.Qg4 piece sac line and whether ...Ne7 holds
Caro Advance: 1.e4 c6 2.d4 d5 3.e5 Bf5. Get the light bishop out before ...e6 locks it in. That is the whole point.
Watch 4.Nf3 e6 5.Be2 with ideas of Nh4 hitting the bishop. Retreat ...Bg6, never ...Bg4 here.
Berlin: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bb5 Nf6 4.O-O Nxe4 5.d4 Nd6 6.Bxc6 dxc6 7.dxe5 Nf5 8.Qxd8+ Kxd8. The endgame.
As White I avoid the Berlin wall. Play 4.d3 instead, keep queens on, slower buildup with c3 and Nbd2.
KIA: Nf3, g3, Bg2, O-O, d3, Nbd2, e4. Same setup against almost anything. Knight to f1 then e3 or g3, then attack the king.
Play the e5 pawn lever timing against the French structure five times
Fried Liver: 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4 Nf6 4.Ng5 d5 5.exd5 Nxd5 6.Nxf7. Fell for it once. Never play 5...Nxd5, play 5...Na5.
5...Na5 6.Bb5+ c6 7.dxc6 bxc6 8.Be2 h6 and the knight retreats. Black is fine, just play accurately.
Lost to Roman Wednesday. Najdorf, English Attack. Got my move order wrong, played ...O-O before ...b5 and got mated.
g4-g5 came too fast. Should have delayed castling and met the pawn storm with ...h5. Fix the move order.
Rematch Roman, same line, with the corrected order
Had R+3 vs R+2 same side, threw away the win. Did not cut the king on the third rank. Lucena needs the bridge.
Rook belongs behind the passed pawn. Always. Drilled it wrong for years. Dvoretsky chapter four.
Lucena position: build the bridge. King to f7, pawn to e7, rook to the fourth rank to block the checks, then promote.
Philidor for the defense: keep the rook on the third rank until the pawn advances, then check from behind.
King and pawn: who has the opposition decides it. Count the squares. Key squares in front of the pawn win.
Solve twenty opposition and triangulation studies on the board, not the screen
Nimzowitsch: prophylaxis is asking what the opponent wants, then stopping it before you do anything else.
Overprotect the strong point. Blockade the passed pawn with a knight. These two ideas show up every game.
Round 1 prep. Likely 1.e4. If Open Sicilian, go Najdorf. If anti-Sicilian Alapin, ...d5 equalizes clean.
Sleep early, no blitz the night before, review only the first six moves of each line
Alapin: 1.e4 c5 2.c3. Hit it with 2...d5 3.exd5 Qxd5 4.d4 Nf6, develop fast, the queen is fine on d5 for now.
Closed Sicilian 2.Nc3 g6: just set up a Dragon structure, ...Bg7, ...d6, ...Nc6, no theory needed.
Ruy Lopez Berlin - critical squares e5 d4. White plays c3.
Sicilian Najdorf - 6.Be3 English attack, kingside pawn storm.
Sicilian Najdorf - 6.Bg5 poisoned pawn line, sharpest.
King's Indian Mar del Plata - f3 g4 kingside break.
Nimzo-Indian Rubinstein - e3 solid, avoids theory.
Queen's Gambit Slav - Meran variation, black hangs a pawn.
Caro-Kann Advance - 3.e5 Bf5 solid black structure.
French Winawer - 3.Nc3 Bb4 dry positional white pressure.
Alekhine's Defense - avoids main lines, provokes overextension.
Rossolimo Sicilian - 3.Bb5 avoids Najdorf theory.
Smith Morra Gambit - sharp anti-Sicilian, active pieces.
London System - solid white repertoire, few surprises.
Bird's Opening - 1.f4 unusual, kingside attack.
Reti Opening - 1.Nf3 flexible white repertoire.
English Symmetrical - solid positional, transposes often.
Ruy Lopez Marshall - black sacs pawn for attack.
Ruy Lopez Zaitsev - modern main line, deep theory.
KID Bayonet Attack - white pushes b4, race on flanks.
Grunfeld Exchange - white gets big center, black attacks.
Grunfeld Russian - solid white positional grind.
Modern Defense - 1...g6 flexible, transposes to Pirc.
Pirc Defense - 1.e4 d6 flexible, weathers e5 pushes.
Scandinavian Defense - 1.e4 d5 direct, solid.
Petroff Defense - solid drawing weapon, symmetrical.
Berlin Wall Ruy Lopez - Kramnik's fortress, endgame.
Bishop Endgames - two bishops win vs bishop plus knight typically.
Rook Endgames - Lucena position, key winning technique.
Rook Endgames - Philidor position, key drawing technique.
King and Pawn Endgame - opposition and key squares.
Knight Endgames - short-range, king activity paramount.
Bishops of opposite color - drawish tendencies, attack chances.
Queen vs Rook endgame - Q wins with correct technique.
Fischer Random Chess - piece coordination different.
Time management - never below 30 seconds under G/60+30.
Post-mortem review - always play through in silence first.
Preparation notes - study opponents' games before tournament.
Tournament prep - sleep, hydration, opening review only.
Blitz training - 3+2 daily on Lichess, avoids rating drift.
Puzzle training - Chess.com puzzle rush, keep pattern sharp.
Middlegame plans - IQP structures, minority attacks.
Strategic themes - weak squares, outposts, color complexes.
Prophylaxis - Nimzowitsch, restrain first, then blockade.
Two weaknesses principle - Dvoretsky, alternate targets.
King safety - open files near king are critical.
Piece activity over material - trade pieces to activate king.
Tempo in endgame - triangulation, zugzwang.
Fortress construction - narrow king, blockade, no pawn breaks.
Rook activity - passive rooks lose endgames.
Pawn breaks - single most important middlegame concept.
Central control - classical vs hypermodern approaches.
Development - piece before pawn, never move same piece twice.
Castling - almost always kingside except positional need.
Trade theory - trade when ahead in material or in space.
Weakness principle - target squares one color, then other.
Isolated pawn - dynamic potential vs long-term weakness.
Backward pawn - target for attack, blockade square weak.
Passed pawn - most valuable in endgame, push relentlessly.
Doubled pawns - not always bad, can control files.
Fianchetto structures - long diagonal bishop, king safety.
Space advantage - suffocate opponent, avoid trades.
Coordination - pieces together, not scattered.
Initiative - keep opponent reactive, never let them dictate.
Concrete calculation - candidates method, Kotov's tree.
Positional evaluation - Steinitz elements, weak squares.
Dynamic factors - development, initiative, king safety.
Static factors - pawn structure, piece placement, material.
Long-term plans - identify at move 15-20, execute over 20 moves.
Middle game to endgame transition - trade activity for material.
Openings for me - Ruy Lopez white, Najdorf black, Nimzo Indian.
Fall Classic prep - study Kaminski's last 30 games.
Spring Open prep - Roman's opening choice as white unpredictable.
